CPC Discussion topics.value:
discussed what skills they would like to cover in their sessions. their future goals etc. | During this session I spoke a little bit about my own background, how I chose my career, and where I am now. We then talked about why Devin and Jabez were interested in the directions they were considering and some additional majors that fit their areas of interest. 

Today we started the Problem Solving learning topic by focusing in on problem identification. We defined a problem as a barrier or obstacle that prevents you from moving forward. Examples range from a disagreement with a friend to an electrical circuit that is broken. We might identify we have a problem in different ways depending on the area that the problem is in, but often the problem can be identified by something that isn't working, you can't get by (ex. the wall in between you and your destination), or something that just feels incorrect. Once we know we have a problem (or think we have one), we can drill down and search for the root cause (we'll cover this in session 3). We also discussed that problems can be seen in a positive light, as we can learn from them, and that we can also be the problem by standing in our own way or by other actions that we take. Recognizing this can sometimes be challenging, but one good way to do it is to ask, "how did I positively or negatively impact what's happening?" Asked another way, "what would have been different if I had done something differently?" We capped this topic off by identifying problems that Devin and Jabez had faced. 

Lastly, we took some time for some questions, and I made a suggestion that one way to learn is through journaling. | The skill we learned today was Problem Impact Assessment. 
Breaking this down, when we refer to a problem, the impact is the answer to one of a few questions: - What cannot be done as a result of the problem? (ex. because of the software error, we cannot sell to our customers)- What does the problem slow down? (ex. slow cash registers mean we have a long line and it is going to take a longer time for people to check out)- What does the problem stop us from doing? (similar to the first question--some people respond to one over the other)- How does it make us feel? (ex. certain actions may have damaged a relationship and you now feel unsure about how you will rectify it and upset) 

The impacts of a problem are often negative, but they can also be positive. For example, while we are not currently able to sell, our manufacturing is catching up on older orders (backorders). For another impact example, ask yourself what the result of not doing what your parents tell you to do is? Or what would happen if you didn't pay your electric bill? Your answers to the first will range,  but the second is pretty clear--don't pay enough times and your electricity could be shut off.

During the session, we worked through a few challenging scenarios. (1) If I encounter the error, "Order parsing cannot be completed." I don't necessarily need to know right off the bat what it means. The impact will help me get that. Asking, "What can you not do as a result of the problem?" We might learn that orders cannot be taken from customers (or in other words, we can't sell). The next question you ask might get to the severity of the problem, "Are all of you customers impacted?" If the answer is yes, this is a very large problem; if the answer is half, still big but it provides us some information that will be valuable to find the cause. If it is only one customer, it may not be a large problem--unless, for example, it is your biggest customer. For a company whose biggest customer is Walmart, and they can't handle their order, that could be catastrophic to their business.(2) If our monitoring tools are showing a low electrical voltage entering the building, the following question is the same: "What can you not do as a result of the problem?" The answer, in this case, might be, that they are a hospital and their imaging equipment is out of order. If you aren't sure what this means, a follow-up question is needed. Think about the context of what we are talking about--a hospital serves patients. So your follow-up to gain additional context might be, how does this impact the patient. 

This skill takes practice and is not something you are proficient with overnight. The best thing you can do to practice is to ask questions and follow-up questions. The only bad question is the one not asked, especially when you are learning.

This skill can be used in any role. But here are three examples:- De-Escalation Architect (my role): Use to prioritize the problem we handle first and to provide information that might help us solve the problem.- Support Engineer (any kind of engineering): Use to solve problems for a customer.- Developer / Engineer (any kind of engineering): Use to understand a customer's needs and create a solution that meets the need.

We also discussed our passions a bit. We'll get into that more in other sessions. | Today we started out with a check-in and then discussed the skills you should have on your resumes by now. We then finished the problem-solving topic off with a focus on a root cause analysis skill called the 5-whys. Here is an overview of the 5-Whys topic:

Now that we understand there is a problem and what its impact is, we need to focus on finding its root cause, which lets us find a solution. A root cause is a true reason that the problem happened. A tree stays up because its roots hold it there; if its roots aren't strong, the tree can fall. This is a good way to think about the root cause. The first example we discussed was that you might be late to school because you forgot to set your alarm the night before, but that happened because you stayed up late for whatever reason and fell asleep. The reason for this (ex. you were talking to your friends, you were doing homework because you worked several hours after work) is part of the root cause.

We discussed the same examples using the 5-whys. The basic idea of the 5 whys is that if you ask why enough times, whether it is 3 times or 15, you will eventually get to the root cause. It is often a little more complex than just asking why. In order to answer each why, some level of analysis might be needed. We discussed this for each of your examples. 

5-Whys is useful whether you are trying to find why there is a disagreement or whether you are working on a complex engineering problem. Debugging of code was one example we discussed because just like the 5 whys, we are working backward from the problem to the root cause. 

You can find the cases we discussed in the attached PDF. I chose to PDF as any mobile device or computer should be able to open it.

As we wrapped up, we discussed what we will cover over the next 6 sessions; we also discussed questions including one about how to handle part-time work with Speakhire. The core point of my answer was that if it something you want/need to do, you should think about how you want to manage your time since you have school work and chores at home on top of that job. I forgot to mention that I had a job in high school and made this work along with being active in a high school club--so it is doable depending on how many hours you work (and there are limits to how many hours you can work at your age as well). | We started our session with a brief check-in and then rolled in to our skill conversation for the week, which took most of the session.

This week had a conversation about why the different areas you are interested in for your career. The goal here was to start to think about the direction we may want to head so that we can move toward choosing the skills we want to learn. We discussed that even if we choose a direction as an example right now, it does not lock us in to that career choice. In fact, it is common for people to change their directions throughout their careers--a process I am going through right now. So if you choose something to focus on here, it isn't set in stone. 

Once we have a direction in mind, we can start to look at what skills we need to get there. 

Here is what we included in the conversation. I am omitting the answers as they are between us.

Jabez - I asked you these questions and more:
- How did you know you were interested in how buildings work and the systems that keep them running? What brought you to have that interest?
- How do you feel about civil engineering -- bridges, roads, other infrastructure?
- Do you like to draw or create things?
- Does creating things excite you?
- Do you have ideas when you see something and think, "I would have done that differently?"
- How are you at math? Science?
- Are you detail-oriented? Do you find yourself focusing on small details?
- Which do you like more: individual work or team work?

Devin - I asked you these questions and more:
- I know you mentioned video games as a reason you became interested in computer engineering. What brought you connect the two? Had you build your own PC? Wanted to build a game?
- When you started your coding class, what surprised you most about it? 
- What do you like about your coding class? What do you dislike?
- What do you find easiest about coding? What do you find hardest?
- Do you like to create things? 
- Do you like to work on your own more or with other people to figure out problems?
- Do you like to be detailed?

Next week we will discuss your answers and talk about what goal you might like to work toward and what skills you need to reach the goal.

Great job today! | We started out by talking about what I heard from you last time and then talked about the different career areas / job titles and majors that are associated with the areas you are looking at. We also talked about how you can start getting an idea of what skills are involved in the jobs that you are interested in so that we can discuss how to start learning them or get to know more about them. Using a simple Google search, such as, "game developer jobs," Google will allow you to see current job openings. We looked at software engineering job description and a game developer job description and showed you how in many cases the job descriptions are pretty detailed in telling you exactly what skills they want you to have. This shows up under the requirements section of the job posting. The other thing you can look for is companies that excite you--we talked about Epic Games. A Google search of "jobs at epic games" brings up jobs like "Gameplay programmer." I'd suggest spending about 20 minutes or so looking at job postings and making a list of the common skills. If you write those down, we can discuss them next week and we'll talk about where to learn those skills.
@Jabez - The same types of searches will work for you, with different keywords. Ex. Refrigeration Engineer jobs, Mechanical engineer jobs -- these both fit some of the areas you might want to look at the skills in.

Here's what I put together for each of you prior to the session.
--Devin--
Careers
- Programmer
- Developer
- Software Application Engineer
- Database administrator
- Computer System Engineer
- Computer System Analyst
- Web developer
- Business Intelligence Analyst
- Software quality assurance engineer
- AR/VR engineer
Game programmer
Cyber Security Engineer
Computer Engineer (ex. CPU Physical Design Engineer, BIOSengineer) 

Education
- Information Technology
- Computer Science
- Software Engineering (not just coding, but also testing,etc.)
- Computer Engineering / Electrical Engineering
- Game and Interactive Media / Game Development

--Jabez - I've filled some extra detail in here for you since you weren't with us--
Careers
- Civil Engineer – Plan, Design, Construct, Maintain structures such as buildings, roads, bridges, dams
- Building Engineer / Architectural Engineer – application of engineering / technology to building design and construction, high performing buildings that are sustainable, resilient, etc.
- Building Maintenance Engineer -  Responsible for mechanical and structural up keep of a building and ensuring the building is safe and running smoothly
- Structural Engineer – Part of Civil engineering. Design structures. Work as a consultant.
Electrical Engineer – Power systems
Quality Engineer / Six Sigma / Lean – focused on manufacturing 
Refrigeration Engineer (which you have mentioned and are targeting) 

Education
- Mechanical Engineering (good for quality, maintenance, engines, HVAC, robotics, tools) 
- Electrical Engineering (automotive, aerospace, oil/gas, medical, electrical, energy engineering jobs)
- Civil Engineering (structural engineer related to construction, geotechnical engineer, environmental engineer, marine engineer, design engineer, water engineer, civil engineer; build everything from roads, to buildings, to tunnels or dams, to bridges, to water systems and sewage treatment)
- Construction Engineering (closely related to civil engineering)
- Architectural Engineering (acoustic consulting, architecture, bridge engineer, building products, building retrofit, carbon management, construction project management systems like power generation) | Today, after catching up, we followed up on Wednesday's conversation about finding skills you need to develop. I was glad to see that you took some time to look at job postings related to game development. The requirements you found were: a degree in computer science, communications skills, knowledge of unreal engine, C++, and working together. These are reflective of the skills we saw when we looked last week. 

We talked about these skills and other growth areas that will impact gaming such as virtual reality and augmented reality. Gaming using these technologies is also useful in some business cases. For example, learning topics that are best practiced by being in the situation. There are military virtual reality foreign language applications that allow soldiers to learn based on the situation the simulation places them in. 

In our topic this week we targeted the C++ skill and talked about two key points: 

1. Where can you learn C++ beyond your coding class at school. There are many options, but one example of a free one is code.org. This is a good way to develop your skills in logic and learn about the structure of code. Additionally, you can find courses at Udemy and Coursera that are often inexpensive (Coursera is a subscription that can be canceled at any time). A good example is this course on Udemy focused on C++ and Unreal Engine.

2. How picking a project can allow you to grow your skills. I showed you a video of an example, and while most people don't have the space or the capital resources to pull this off, it was a good example of someone learning something and applying it through a project.  You can do the same thing--by learning and then picking a project to apply it to. You could even do it collaboratively. For example, if you have friends who like animation, you may be able to help each other learn and apply the skills to make a game. | After our check-in, we talked about your learning project. I was really impressed that you took the step of taking what we discussed and starting to apply it. Programming a Tetris app would be a really good challenge and allow you to learn bundles. As we discussed, you could consider honing your logic skills first by developing a text-based choose your own adventure app. That will help get you ready. Remember we talked about figuring out what skills you need to get where you want to go. You will need to build those skills up, and that is the best way to learn to program. Learn a skill, build on it by learning another, and continue. 

Our main topic today was networking. We talked about how networking is really making a connection. It's a conversation, but the first thing you really need to know is that it is very much about the first impression and then using that to continue developing a relationship. 

Networking is a two-way street. Both people are getting something out of it. Think of it this way, in some cases, it may be as simple as you want a job and that person wants a great employee. Networking is not one and done. Often we meet one person who leads us to another, and if you were to eventually draw out your connections it might look like a tree (or a spider web if many knew each other). 

Networking can happen anywhere: an event, a get-together with family friends (someday), a classroom, etc. In-person networking starts with a firm handshake (though our culture may change on this post-covid) and an introduction. You may have someone introducing you, or you may be on your own and introduce yourself.

Virtual networking can happen in so many ways. We talked about an example of networking on LinkedIn that I had this morning. So social media platforms have opportunities for networking (remember our discussion about being mindful of what you post as it stays with you). We also may connect with people through email directly or be introduced by someone else on social or email. Introductions work a lot in our favor as it puts some additional credibility behind us.

When you write that message, there's a big difference between an informal email that you might write to a friend and a professional email. These are worth putting time into and crafting. a good example is:

Dear Ms. New-Network:
I am a high school sophomore at Queens Preparatory Academy, in Queens, NY, and received your contact information from Joe Schmo. Joe mentioned that you are a Lead Developer at EA Sports. 

I am an avid gamer and started coding this year. Combining these two interests, I am interested to find out more about what it is like to be a gaming developer. I'm considering a computer science or software engineering major in college and would love to hear your insights. 

Would you be willing to have a conversation with me about your career?

Thank you,
Devin Rhodie | talked about the resume. This week had a conversation about why the different areas you are interested in for your career. The goal here was to start to think about the direction we may want to head so that we can move toward choosing the skills we want to learn. We discussed that even if we choose a direction as an example right now, it does not lock us in to that career choice. In fact, it is common for people to change their directions throughout their careers--a process I am going through right now. So if you choose something to focus on here, it isn't set in stone. 

Once we have a direction in mind, we can start to look at what skills we need to get there. 

Here is what we included in the conversation. I am omitting the answers as they are between us.

Jabez - I asked you these questions and more:
- How did you know you were interested in how buildings work and the systems that keep them running? What brought you to have that interest?
- How do you feel about civil engineering -- bridges, roads, other infrastructure?
- Do you like to draw or create things?
- Does creating things excite you?
- Do you have ideas when you see something and think, "I would have done that differently?"
- How are you at math? Science?
- Are you detail-oriented? Do you find yourself focusing on small details?
- Which do you like more: individual work or team work?

Devin - I asked you these questions and more:
- I know you mentioned video games as a reason you became interested in computer engineering. What brought you connect the two? Had you build your own PC? Wanted to build a game?
- When you started your coding class, what surprised you most about it? 
- What do you like about your coding class? What do you dislike?
- What do you find easiest about coding? What do you find hardest?
- Do you like to create things? 
- Do you like to work on your own more or with other people to figure out problems?
- Do you like to be detailed?

Next week we will discuss your answers and talk about what goal you might like to work toward and what skills you need to reach the goal.

We also talked about how you can start getting an idea of what skills are involved in the jobs that you are interested in so that we can discuss how to start learning them or get to know more about them. Using a simple Google search, such as, "game developer jobs," Google will allow you to see current job openings. I was glad to see that you took some time to look at job postings related to game development. The requirements you found were: a degree in computer science, communications skills, knowledge of unreal engine, C++, and working together. These are reflective of the skills we saw when we looked last week. 

We talked about these skills and other growth areas that will impact gaming such as virtual reality and augmented reality. Gaming using these technologies is also useful in some business cases. For example, learning topics that are best practiced by being in the situation. There are military virtual reality foreign language applications that allow soldiers to learn based on the situation the simulation places them in. 

In our topic this week we targeted the C++ skill and talked about two key points: 

1. Where can you learn C++ beyond your coding class at school. There are many options, but one example of a free one is code.org. This is a good way to develop your skills in logic and learn about the structure of code. Additionally, you can find courses at Udemy and Coursera that are often inexpensive (Coursera is a subscription that can be canceled at any time). A good example is this course on Udemy focused on C++ and Unreal Engine.

2. How picking a project can allow you to grow your skills. I showed you a video of an example, and while most people don't have the space or the capital resources to pull this off, it was a good example of someone learning something and applying it through a project.  You can do the same thing--by learning and then picking a project to apply it to. You could even do it collaboratively. For example, if you have friends who like animation, you may be able to help each other learn and apply the skills to make a game. | After our check-in, we talked about your learning project. I was really impressed that you took the step of taking what we discussed and starting to apply it. Programming a Tetris app would be a really good challenge and allow you to learn bundles. As we discussed, you could consider honing your logic skills first by developing a text-based choose your own adventure app. That will help get you ready. Remember we talked about figuring out what skills you need to get where you want to go. You will need to build those skills up, and that is the best way to learn to program. Learn a skill, build on it by learning another, and continue. 

Our main topic today was networking. We talked about how networking is really making a connection. It's a conversation, but the first thing you really need to know is that it is very much about the first impression and then using that to continue developing a relationship. 

Networking is a two-way street. Both people are getting something out of it. Think of it this way, in some cases, it may be as simple as you want a job and that person wants a great employee. Networking is not one and done. Often we meet one person who leads us to another, and if you were to eventually draw out your connections it might look like a tree (or a spider web if many knew each other). 

Networking can happen anywhere: an event, a get-together with family friends (someday), a classroom, etc. In-person networking starts with a firm handshake (though our culture may change on this post-covid) and an introduction. You may have someone introducing you, or you may be on your own and introduce yourself.

Virtual networking can happen in so many ways. We talked about an example of networking on LinkedIn that I had this morning. So social media platforms have opportunities for networking (remember our discussion about being mindful of what you post as it stays with you). We also may connect with people through email directly or be introduced by someone else on social or email. Introductions work a lot in our favor as it puts some additional credibility behind us.

When you write that message, there's a big difference between an informal email that you might write to a friend and a professional email. These are worth putting time into and crafting. a good example is:

Dear Ms. New-Network:
I am a high school sophomore at Queens Preparatory Academy, in Queens, NY, and received your contact information from Joe Schmo. Joe mentioned that you are a Lead Developer at EA Sports. 

I am an avid gamer and started coding this year. Combining these two interests, I am interested to find out more about what it is like to be a gaming developer. I'm considering a computer science or software engineering major in college and would love to hear your insights. 

Would you be willing to have a conversation with me about your career?

Thank you,
